addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- compound-engineering:design:design-implementation-reviewer: Visually compares live UI implementation against Figma designs and provides detailed feedback on discrepancies. Use after writing or modifying HTML/CSS/React components to verify design fidelity. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:design-iterator: Iteratively refines UI design through N screenshot-analyze-improve cycles. Use PROACTIVELY when design changes aren't coming together after 1-2 attempts, or when user requests iterative refinement. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:design:figma-design-sync: Detects and fixes visual differences between a web implementation and its Figma design. Use iteratively when syncing implementation to match Figma specs. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:docs:ankane-readme-writer: Creates or updates README files following Ankane-style template for Ruby gems. Use when writing gem documentation with imperative voice, concise prose, and standard section ordering. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:best-practices-researcher: Researches and synthesizes external best practices, documentation, and examples for any technology or framework. Use when you need industry standards, community conventions, or implementation guidance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:framework-docs-researcher: Gathers comprehensive documentation and best practices for frameworks, libraries, or dependencies. Use when you need official docs, version-specific constraints, or implementation patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:git-history-analyzer: Performs archaeological analysis of git history to trace code evolution, identify contributors, and understand why code patterns exist. Use when you need historical context for code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:issue-intelligence-analyst: Fetches and analyzes GitHub issues to surface recurring themes, pain patterns, and severity trends. Use when understanding a project's issue landscape, analyzing bug patterns for ideation, or summarizing what users are reporting. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:learnings-researcher: Searches docs/solutions/ for relevant past solutions by frontmatter metadata. Use before implementing features or fixing problems to surface institutional knowledge and prevent repeated mistakes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:research:repo-research-analyst: Conducts thorough research on repository structure, documentation, conventions, and implementation patterns. Use when onboarding to a new codebase or understanding project conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:agent-native-reviewer: Reviews code to ensure agent-native parity — any action a user can take, an agent can also take. Use after adding UI features, agent tools, or system prompts. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:architecture-strategist: Analyzes code changes from an architectural perspective for pattern compliance and design integrity. Use when reviewing PRs, adding services, or evaluating structural refactors. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:code-simplicity-reviewer: Final review pass to ensure code is as simple and minimal as possible. Use after implementation is complete to identify YAGNI violations and simplification opportunities. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-integrity-guardian: Reviews database migrations, data models, and persistent data code for safety. Use when checking migration safety, data constraints, transaction boundaries, or privacy compliance. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:data-migration-expert: Validates data migrations, backfills, and production data transformations against reality. Use when PRs involve ID mappings, column renames, enum conversions, or schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:deployment-verification-agent: Produces Go/No-Go deployment checklists with SQL verification queries, rollback procedures, and monitoring plans. Use when PRs touch production data, migrations, or risky data changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:dhh-rails-reviewer: Brutally honest Rails code review from DHH's perspective. Use when reviewing Rails code for anti-patterns, JS framework contamination, or violations of Rails conventions. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:julik-frontend-races-reviewer: Reviews JavaScript and Stimulus code for race conditions, timing issues, and DOM lifecycle problems. Use after implementing or modifying frontend controllers or async UI code. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-python-reviewer: Reviews Python code with an extremely high quality bar for Pythonic patterns, type safety, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Python modules. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-rails-reviewer: Reviews Rails code with an extremely high quality bar for conventions, clarity, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new Rails components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:kieran-typescript-reviewer: Reviews TypeScript code with an extremely high quality bar for type safety, modern patterns, and maintainability. Use after implementing features, modifying code, or creating new TypeScript components. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:pattern-recognition-specialist: Analyzes code for design patterns, anti-patterns, naming conventions, and duplication. Use when checking codebase consistency or verifying new code follows established patterns. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:performance-oracle: Analyzes code for performance bottlenecks, algorithmic complexity, database queries, memory usage, and scalability. Use after implementing features or when performance concerns arise. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:schema-drift-detector: Detects unrelated schema.rb changes in PRs by cross-referencing against included migrations. Use when reviewing PRs with database schema changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:review:security-sentinel: Performs security audits for vulnerabilities, input validation, auth/authz, hardcoded secrets, and OWASP compliance. Use when reviewing code for security issues or before deployment. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:bug-reproduction-validator: Systematically reproduces and validates bug reports to confirm whether reported behavior is an actual bug. Use when you receive a bug report or issue that needs verification. (Tools: All tools)
- compound-engineering:workflow:lint: Use this agent when you need to run linting and code quality checks on Ruby and ERB files. Run before pushing to origin. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:pr-comment-resolver: Addresses PR review comments by implementing requested changes and reporting resolutions. Use when code review feedback needs to be resolved with code changes. (Tools: All tools)

- compound-engineering:workflow:spec-flow-analyzer: Analyzes specifications and feature descriptions for user flow completeness and gap identification. Use when a spec, plan, or feature description needs flow analysis, edge case discovery, or requirements validation. (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- code-changes: Orchestration workflow for any task that ends in code changes: issue analysis, pull request review, feature implementation, bug fixes, refactors, or fleshing out an idea. MUST be invoked at the start of such a task, before reading or writing any code. Defines how to analyze first, gate on user approval, plan, pick the right executor model, delegate and supervise subagents, verify, and deliver.
- context7-mcp: This skill should be used when the user asks about libraries, frameworks, API references, or needs code examples. Activates for setup questions, code generation involving libraries, or mentions of specific frameworks like React, Vue, Next.js, Prisma, Supabase, etc.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- conventional-commit: Workflow for generating conventional commit messages following the Conventional Commits specification. MUST be invoked every time a commit is created. Guides construction of standardized commit messages with correct type, scope, description, body, and footer.
- golang: Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing, reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.
- markdown: Markdown formatting rules for this project. Apply when writing or editing any .md or .mdx file, including documentation and website content.
- powershell: PowerShell cmdlet conventions for this project. Apply when writing or reviewing any .ps1 or module file.
- vale-user-facing-text: Enforce Vale prose checks on any user-facing text the agent creates or edits — comments, documentation, status messages, release notes, READMEs, or any explanatory content that users will read. Always use this skill when producing or changing prose, even when Vale is not explicitly requested.
- writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- compound-engineering:agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- compound-engineering:agent-native-architecture: Build applications where agents are first-class citizens. Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop.
- compound-engineering:andrew-kane-gem-writer: This skill should be used when writing Ruby gems following Andrew Kane's proven patterns and philosophy. It applies when creating new Ruby gems, refactoring existing gems, designing gem APIs, or when clean, minimal, production-ready Ruby library code is needed. Triggers on requests like "create a gem", "write a Ruby library", "design a gem API", or mentions of Andrew Kane's style.
- compound-engineering:ce-brainstorm: Explore requirements and approaches through collaborative dialogue before writing a right-sized requirements document and planning implementation. Use for feature ideas, problem framing, when the user says 'let's brainstorm', or when they want to think through options before deciding what to build. Also use when a user describes a vague or ambitious feature request, asks 'what should we build', 'help me think through X', presents a problem with multiple valid solutions, or seems unsure about scope or direction — even if they don't explicitly ask to brainstorm.
- compound-engineering:ce-compound: Document a recently solved problem to compound your team's knowledge
- compound-engineering:ce-ideate: Generate and critically evaluate grounded improvement ideas for the current project. Use when asking what to improve, requesting idea generation, exploring surprising improvements, or wanting the AI to proactively suggest strong project directions before brainstorming one in depth. Triggers on phrases like 'what should I improve', 'give me ideas', 'ideate on this project', 'surprise me with improvements', 'what would you change', or any request for AI-generated project improvement suggestions rather than refining the user's own idea.
- compound-engineering:ce-plan: Transform feature descriptions into well-structured project plans following conventions
- compound-engineering:ce-review: Perform exhaustive code reviews using multi-agent analysis, ultra-thinking, and worktrees
- compound-engineering:ce-work: Execute work plans efficiently while maintaining quality and finishing features
- compound-engineering:claude-permissions-optimizer: Optimize Claude Code permissions by finding safe Bash commands from session history and auto-applying them to settings.json. Can run from any coding agent but targets Claude Code specifically. Use when experiencing permission fatigue, too many permission prompts, wanting to optimize permissions, or needing to set up allowlists. Triggers on "optimize permissions", "reduce permission prompts", "allowlist commands", "too many permission prompts", "permission fatigue", "permission setup", or complaints about clicking approve too often.
- compound-engineering:deepen-plan: Enhance a plan with parallel research agents for each section to add depth, best practices, and implementation details
- compound-engineering:dhh-rails-style: This skill should be used when writing Ruby and Rails code in DHH's distinctive 37signals style. It applies when writing Ruby code, Rails applications, creating models, controllers, or any Ruby file. Triggers on Ruby/Rails code generation, refactoring requests, code review, or when the user mentions DHH, 37signals, Basecamp, HEY, or Campfire style. Embodies REST purity, fat models, thin controllers, Current attributes, Hotwire patterns, and the "clarity over cleverness" philosophy.
- compound-engineering:document-review: This skill should be used to refine requirements or plan documents before proceeding to the next workflow step. It applies when a requirements document or plan document exists and the user wants to improve it.
- compound-engineering:dspy-ruby: Build type-safe LLM applications with DSPy.rb — Ruby's programmatic prompt framework with signatures, modules, agents, and optimization. Use when implementing predictable AI features, creating LLM signatures and modules, configuring language model providers, building agent systems with tools, optimizing prompts, or testing LLM-powered functionality in Ruby applications.
- compound-engineering:every-style-editor: This skill should be used when reviewing or editing copy to ensure adherence to Every's style guide. It provides a systematic line-by-line review process for grammar, punctuation, mechanics, and style guide compliance.
- compound-engineering:feature-video: Record a video walkthrough of a feature and add it to the PR description. Use when a PR needs a visual demo for reviewers, when the user asks to demo a feature, create a PR video, record a walkthrough, show what changed visually, or add a video to a pull request.
- compound-engineering:gemini-imagegen: This skill should be used when generating and editing images using the Gemini API (Nano Banana Pro). It applies when creating images from text prompts, editing existing images, applying style transfers, generating logos with text, creating stickers, product mockups, or any image generation/manipulation task. Supports text-to-image, image editing, multi-turn refinement, and composition from multiple reference images.
- compound-engineering:git-worktree: This skill manages Git worktrees for isolated parallel development. It handles creating, listing, switching, and cleaning up worktrees with a simple interactive interface, following KISS principles.
- compound-engineering:proof: Create, edit, comment on, and share markdown documents via Proof's web API and local bridge. Use when asked to "proof", "share a doc", "create a proof doc", "comment on a document", "suggest edits", "review in proof", or when given a proofeditor.ai URL.
- compound-engineering:rclone: Upload, sync, and manage files across cloud storage providers using rclone. Use when uploading files (images, videos, documents) to S3, Cloudflare R2, Backblaze B2, Google Drive, Dropbox, or any S3-compatible storage. Triggers on "upload to S3", "sync to cloud", "rclone", "backup files", "upload video/image to bucket", or requests to transfer files to remote storage.
- compound-engineering:reproduce-bug: Systematically reproduce and investigate a bug from a GitHub issue. Use when the user provides a GitHub issue number or URL for a bug they want reproduced or investigated.
- compound-engineering:resolve-todo-parallel: Resolve all pending CLI todos using parallel processing, compound on lessons learned, then clean up completed todos.
- compound-engineering:test-browser: Run browser tests on pages affected by current PR or branch
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:frontend-slides: Create stunning, animation-rich HTML presentations from scratch or by converting PowerPoint files. Use when the user wants to build a presentation, convert a PPT/PPTX to web, or create slides for a talk/pitch. Helps non-designers discover their aesthetic through visual exploration rather than abstract choices.
- anthropic-skills:itdepends-offering: Use this skill whenever Jan (IT depends) needs to create a professional offering, proposal, quote, or commercial document as a Word (.docx) file. This skill extends the base docx skill with IT depends brand styling: the company logo, clean sans-serif typography, minimal layout with light gray label rows, and structured sections (scope, pricing, timeline, terms). Trigger on phrases like "create an offering", "make a proposal", "write a quote document", "draft a commercial proposal", or "create a Word offering for [client]". Always use this skill (not the generic docx skill) when the output is an IT depends branded offering or proposal.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:proposal-iteration-agent: Use this skill to start an interactive proposal development session. The agent asks intelligent, context-aware questions to gather all necessary information for crafting a professional proposal: client details, scope of work, pricing strategy, timeline, deliverables, and terms. The agent iterates with you, refines content, and maintains a structured proposal framework that can be exported to the itdepends-offering skill once finalized. Trigger on: "start a proposal",  "help me build a proposal", "iterate on proposal content", "gather proposal requirements", or "let's develop a proposal together".
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:spilberg-docs-style: Use this style guide whenever creating a Word document (.docx) for Spilberg — reports, technical documentation, assessments, proposals, or any client-facing deliverable that should look like it came from Spilberg (the IT Development & Testing staffing & consulting brand, part of Vibe Group). Trigger on phrases like "Spilberg report", "Spilberg document", "make this look like Spilberg", "branded for Spilberg", or whenever the user is producing a deliverable to share with Spilberg's clients or candidates. This skill defines Spilberg's brand colors, typography, logo usage, and full-page layout (including the signature green footer wordmark) so every document is instantly identifiable as Spilberg's own. Always combine with the base docx skill for the underlying docx-js mechanics.
- anthropic-skills:vibe-group-docs-style: Use this style guide when the user asks to generate a document for Vibe Group.
- anthropic-skills:writing-clearly-and-concisely: Apply whenever writing content a human will read — emails, documents, reports, documentation, messages, UI copy, commit messages, explanations, or any other prose. Applies Strunk's timeless rules for clearer, stronger, more professional writing.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues which of the issues are feasible to solve?

input.command: gh issue list --repo JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh --state open --limit 100 --json number,title,labels,comments,createdAt,updatedAt --template '{{range .}}{{.number}}\t{{.title}}\t{{range .labels}}{{.name}},{{end}}\t{{.comments}}\t{{.createdAt}}\t{{.updatedAt}}\n{{end}}'

content.content:
7594\tproject segment - multiple project files\t🚀 feat,\t[]\t2026-06-20T21:01:22Z\t2026-06-20T21:01:30Z\n7572\tAllow palette colors to use Go templates\t🤩 enhancement,\t[]\t2026-06-10T02:56:34Z\t2026-06-10T02:56:34Z\n7531\t[Feature request] Multi-level extends / proxy config file for switching palettes\t🚀 feat,\t[]\t2026-05-17T18:48:21Z\t2026-05-17T18:48:21Z\n7427\trepaint_prompt function for other shells\t🚀 feat,\t[map[author:map[login:JanDeDobbeleer] authorAssociation:OWNER body:@Ceedrich other shells have this functionality out of the box no? createdAt:2026-03-27T10:16:00Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc7223Nc includesCreatedEdit:false isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427#issuecomment-4141577052 viewerDidAuthor:true] map[author:map[login:Ceedrich] authorAssociation:NONE body:I looked into `zsh` (since it's what I'm using) and it offers the widget `reset-prompt`. So, with a minimal pure zsh config (run with `zsh -df` then `source` the config):
```sh
# zshrc
PROMPT="%*"
bindkey '^B' reset-prompt
```

pressing `C-B` will successfully reload the default zsh prompt.
However, using a minimal `oh-my-posh` config:
```sh
# zshrc
eval $(oh-my-posh init zsh --config minimal.toml)
bindkey '^B' reset-prompt
```

```
# minimal.toml
"$schema" = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/main/themes/schema.json"

version = 4
[[blocks]]
alignment = "left"
type = "prompt"

[[blocks.segments]]
type = "time"
style = "plain"
[blocks.segments.options]
time_format = "15:04:05"
```

pressing `C-B` does not change the prompt. So yes, there is a builtin way to reset the prompt but it does not seem to work with `oh-my-posh`.

Update:
Consider the following zshrc. This works, i.e. pressing `C-B` resets the prompt.
```sh
autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook

set_prompt() {
  PS1="$(date +%H:%M:%S)"
}

update_prompt() {
  set_prompt
  zle reset-prompt
}

zle -N update_prompt

bindkey '^B' update_prompt

add-zsh-hook precmd set_prompt
```

I believe the `reset_prompt` widget does not trigger the `precmd` hook which is used by `oh-my-posh` to (re-)render the prompt. createdAt:2026-03-27T11:06:46Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc7232eT includesCreatedEdit:true isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427#issuecomment-4141836179 viewerDidAuthor:false] map[author:map[login:JanDeDobbeleer] authorAssociation:OWNER body:@Ceedrich alright, what's the use-case for this as it would require a bit of work to get this done. createdAt:2026-03-28T10:47:42Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc73Ovg0 includesCreatedEdit:false isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427#issuecomment-4147836980 viewerDidAuthor:true] map[author:map[login:Ceedrich] authorAssociation:NONE body:I would like to use it to make a vi mode indicator createdAt:2026-03-28T13:59:48Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc73P1PQ includesCreatedEdit:false isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427#issuecomment-4148122576 viewerDidAuthor:false] map[author:map[login:ndrwstn] authorAssociation:NONE body:@JanDeDobbeleer to offer another use, I have matugen re-theming elements based upon my wallpaper in hyprland, including my oh-my-posh prompt. RIght now I have to `enable reload`, and `disable reload` to propagate theme changes. Not really *essential use case* but definitely would be nice. createdAt:2026-04-01T20:24:05Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc74tz-J includesCreatedEdit:false isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427#issuecomment-4172758921 viewerDidAuthor:false]]\t2026-03-27T10:03:16Z\t2026-04-01T20:24:05Z\n6334\tPath segment: Multiple non-consecutive capture groups\t🤩 enhancement,\t[map[author:map[login:JanDeDobbeleer] authorAssociation:OWNER body:I don't mind adding 3, but there needs to be a switch to enable this due to the potential negative impact, even if small. I'll play around with it and see what it does. createdAt:2025-04-01T19:32:55Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc6lIlVB includesCreatedEdit:false isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6334#issuecomment-2770490689 viewerDidAuthor:true] map[author:map[login:Bertie690] authorAssociation:NONE body:Yeah hence why I mentioned making it a toggle (ie config boolean).
I might be able to take a look myself as well - I have some prior experience in Golang (and the code doesn't look too terribly obfuscated). createdAt:2025-04-01T22:51:20Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc6lJ-QO includesCreatedEdit:true isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6334#issuecomment-2770854926 viewerDidAuthor:false] map[author:map[login:kossyrev-bg] authorAssociation:NONE body:Hello, fresh user of oh-my-posh here, and I just want to chime in to say this would be a great feature. I have a complicated, though very predictable structure that I would love to capture within several regexes (or one line) that I can't currently do. Something like
```
prefix/pf_folder_a/sub_folder_a/src/sub_folder_a
prefix/pf_folder_b/sub_folder_c/src/sub_folder_c
```
which I could capture and transform using
```
re:prefix -> icon
re:pf_[^/]+/(([^/]+)/src/\2) -> $2

output: icon/pf_folder_a/sub_folder_a
output: icon/pf_folder_b/sub_folder_c
```
using your proposed "parse all maps on the line" solution (3).

This would be super helpful for saving space in my terminal! createdAt:2025-06-12T15:45:32Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc6w3lCD includesCreatedEdit:false isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6334#issuecomment-2967359619 viewerDidAuthor:false] map[author:map[login:Bertie690] authorAssociation:NONE body:Tt's been a few months and I thought I might try and take a look at things today.

However, it took less than an hour of digging before I encountered a large and fairly painful roadblock - _JSON iteration order_. 
To replace keys in turn, we must loop over those keys in some determinstic order, ideally without checking every single key at once.

# 1: Pre-defined sorting methods
However, sorting the list in ascending or descending alphabetical order no longer work with repeated matchings. 
Since the "suffix" of one key will correspond to the prefix of another, reverse alphabetical order is far from guaranteed  (and in fact extremely likely) to place potential "endpoint" mapped locations before their peers. 

For instance, take a config like this, matching the folder `foo` to `bar` and both `foo/baz` & `bar/baz` to `qux` in both windows and WSL:
```json
{
  "mnt/a": "A://",
  "A://foo": "bar",
  "bar/baz": "qux"
}
```
If done in ascending order, `bar/baz` is checked before `mnt/a`, meaning the config breaks in WSL.
If done in descending order (what we currently do right now), `bar/baz` is matched before `A:/foo`, meaning the config breaks in Windows.

# 2. User-defined Key Order
Given that pre-defining the sort order will have an _extremely_ high likelihood of breaking common patterns in some way or another, my next consideration was letting the user define it themself through order of placed keys.

However, this is made more complicated by the fact that `oh-my-posh` stores its keys within nested maps over whom iteration order is undefined. 
Furthermore, Golang's `json.Unmarshal` is known to [scramble JSON keys](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/27179) during the unmarshaling process itself, making preserving order much more complicated. [encoding/json/v2](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/go-json-experiment/json) isn't production-ready, so our options would boil down to waiting a year or two, implementing an in-house solution to retrieve ordered pairs or implementing a 3rd-party package that does so[^1]. 
(JSON is _technically_ unordered as per the spec, though I doubt that really matters for user-defined data.)

[^1]: Ironically, this is _only_ an issue for JSON. From what I can tell, the currently used YAML and TOML decoders _do_ support ordering of values, which is just perfect... 

# 3. Solutions???

With that aside, there are 2 solutions I can think of:

1. Making the sort order part of the values itself.
Something like this:
```json
  "test": {"pattern": "blah", "order": 1},
  "test2": {"pattern": "asdfg", "order": 2},
  "test3": {"pattern": "qwerty", "order": 3}
```
would allow us to preserve and extract the key order for sorting later.
[^2]

However, this would effectively require widening the scope of the proposal to allow arbitrary mapped path evaluation order (a feature far from intuitive), not to mention I'd rather avoid adding arbitrary syntax for no reason...

Also, how to deal with mixing of ordered and unordered values inside the config (or prevent mixing them altogether) is beyond me.

[^2]: IDK how this would translate to TOML or YAML.

2. Checking everything at once

This promotes ease-of-use at the cost of any semblance of performance. 
Checking every path after each one matches effectively makes the runtime up to `O(n^2)` with the number of mappings (ones which can already consist of fairly lengthy regexes). Some time may be saved given that non-matching mappings will oftentimes fail to match fairly quickly, but it still ain't pretty.

3. Migrate to ordered pairs (`[][2]interface` instead of a map

While this would be flexible, it would _also_ be a royal pain to implement and migrate everything to in a backwards-compatible manner. 

@JanDeDobbeleer Any thoughts or feedback would be greatly appreciated - I am known for being quite good at overlooking things, so a second set of eyes would be greatly appreciated. createdAt:2025-06-14T22:16:17Z id:IC_kwDOCnR4Zc6xOLlt includesCreatedEdit:true isMinimized:false minimizedReason: reactionGroups:[] url:https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6334#issuecomment-2973284717 viewerDidAuthor:false]]\t2025-04-01T15:21:40Z\t2025-06-14T22:17:23Z\n5442\tMore fine-grained controls over color cycle\t🤩 enhancement,\t[]\t2024-08-10T19:47:22Z\t2024-08-10T19:47:22Z\n

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content.content:
project segment - multiple project files

### Code of Conduct

- [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What would you like to see added?

I work on projects with multiple project files (eg. composer.json and package.json in the same directory). Only one of them has all the info in it, and I would like to have a way of showing the right one or all of them. There is currently no setting to tell what file should have priority or to loop over each.

Thank you for your time and this cool project!

---COMMENTS---

content.content: Allow palette colors to use Go templates ### Code of Conduct - [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct ### What would you like to see changed? # Suggested Enhancement Allow palette colors to use Go templates (and also allow `transparent` to be a valid color): ```yaml palette: color1: #xxxxxx color2: #xxxxxx color3: '{{ if CONDITION }}p:color1{{ else }}p:color2{{ end }} ``` # Explanation of Enhancement's Utility ## Desired Behavior Suppose we want the following effect; take note of the _dynamic_ behavior of the pair of ``. E.g. consider the pair of  immediately following the path segment. The desired behavior is: if 1. The Python segment is not present 2. The Git segment is not present 3. The previous command returned with exit code 0 then, the first  has background color `p:green` and foreground color `f:bg0` (the shade of black you see below). It would be _very_ useful (and much more elegant than my current solution) to be able to just specify ```yaml palette: separator_git: '{{ if and (not .Segments.Status.Error) (not (.Segments.Contains "Git")) (not (.Segments.Contains "Python")) }}p:green{{ else if and (.Segments.Status.Error) (not (.Segments.Contains "Git")) (not (.Segments.Contains "Python")) }}p:red{{ else if or (.Segments.Contains "Git")) (.Segments.Contains "Python") }}p:bg0{{ {{ end }}' ``` and correspondingly write the Git segment as ```yaml blocks: - type: prompt alignment: left segments: - template: '  ' foreground: 'p:yellow' type: root style: plain - template: '{{ .UserName }}@{{ .HostName }} ' foreground: 'p:fg' background: 'p:bg0' type: session style: diamond leading_diamond:  trailing_diamond:  - options: style: full template: ' {{ .Path }}' foreground: 'p:blue' background: 'p:bg0' type: path style: diamond leading_diamond:  trailing_diamond:  ``` ## Current Behavior Instead, the current solution I came up with is 1. Put an empty `trailing_diamond` for the Path segment. 2. Insert three Text segments (only one of which renders at any time) corresponding to each of the aforementioned cases. Naturally, this is massively inelegant: ```yaml blocks: - type: prompt alignment: left segments: - template: '  ' foreground: 'p:yellow' type: root style: plain - template: '{{ .UserName }}@{{ .HostName }} ' foreground: 'p:fg' background: 'p:bg0' type: session style: diamond leading_diamond:  trailing_diamond:  - options: style: full template: ' {{ .Path }}' foreground: 'p:blue' background: 'p:bg0' type: path style: diamond leading_diamond:  trailing_diamond: '' # >>> Flex tape for the background of the rightmost  ('trailing_diamond') of the preceding 'type: path' segment >>> # Explanation: if the next segment, which is the 'git' segment, does not render, then `template: '<200b>'` for the first/second segment below so the  with transparent backgrand renders. Meanwhile, `template: ''` for the third segment below so it won't render anything. Conversely, this means that the  will be correctly rendered with the background color 'p:separator', whenever the 'git' segment renders. # Segments 1 and 2 below build the right angled bracket, together with the 'Status' segment, which is green (exit code = 0) and red (exit code != 0) respectively. - template: '{{ if and (not .Segments.Status.Error) (not (.Segments.Contains "Git")) (not (.Segments.Contains "Python")) }}⠀{{ end }}' # [BUGGED as of v29.14.0: ​ results in display bug when manually typing strings into the TTY longer than the TTY's max width. HOTFIX used: braille pattern blank \u2800 (which produces a space) + removal of the original single trailing space from the preceding segment (e.g. path)'s 'template' field] Use the Unicode zero-width space character ​ to force this segment's rendering without affecting the output of the appearance of 'oh-my-posh' background: 'p:bg0' type: text style: diamond leading_diamond: '' trailing_diamond:  - template: '{{ if and (.Segments.Status.Error) (not (.Segments.Contains "Git")) (not (.Segments.Contains "Python")) }}⠀{{ end }}' # [BUGGED as of v29.14.0: ​ results in display bug when manually typing strings into the TTY longer than the TTY's max width. HOTFIX used: braille pattern blank \u2800 (which produces a space) + removal of the original single trailing space from the preceding segment (e.g. path)'s 'template' field] Use the Unicode zero-width space character ​ to force this segment's rendering without affecting the output of the appearance of 'oh-my-posh' background: 'p:bg0' type: text style: diamond leading_diamond: '' trailing_diamond:  - template: '{{ if or (.Segments.Contains "Git") (.Segments.Contains "Python") }}⠀{{ end }}' # [BUGGED as of v29.14.0: ​ results in display bug when manually typing strings into the TTY longer than the TTY's max width. HOTFIX used: braille pattern blank \u2800 (which produces a space) + removal of the original single trailing space from the preceding segment (e.g. path)'s 'template' field] Use the Unicode zero-width space character ​ to force this segment's rendering without affecting the output of the appearance of 'oh-my-posh' background: 'p:bg0' type: text style: diamond leading_diamond: '' trailing_diamond:  # <<< Flex tape for the background of the rightmost  ('trailing_diamond') of the preceding 'type: path' segment <<< ``` The full YAML file can be found at https://github.com/tiagovla/tokyodark.nvim/issues/47 # Version _Oh My Posh_ v29.14.0 ---COMMENTS---

content.content:
[Feature request] Multi-level extends / proxy config file for switching palettes

### Code of Conduct

- [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What would you like to see added?

I would like to see support for multi-level `extends` or a way to create a "proxy" configuration file.

My goal is to have three files:

1. **Config file** (`config.toml`) - contains all blocks, segments, and has `extends = "current.toml"` at the top
2. **Palette file** (`palettes/gruvbox.toml`) - contains ONLY the `[palette]` section
3. **Proxy file** (`current.toml`) - a local, unversioned file that ONLY extends the palette that I'll be using

The desired chain is:
config.toml (main config)
↓ extends
current.toml (proxy, in .gitignore)
↓ extends
palettes/gruvbox.toml (palette only)

---COMMENTS---
==========
More fine-grained controls over color cycle

### Code of Conduct

- [X] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What would you like to see changed?

Hello, I have a problem: 
My prompt has two lines - I want the first line to use a color cycle, but I want the second line, where I have the cursor, to always start with the cycle's first colors, regardless of the first line's state.
It doesn't make much sense that, for example, git status appearing on the first line changes the color of the second line.
Good solutions for this problem are I think these:

- allow separate color cycle for each block
- OR add block property that resets the cycle from start
- AND/OR add property that excludes a block from the cycle

---COMMENTS---

3. **Project segment with multiple files (issue 7594)**: find the "project" segment implementation (likely src/segments/project.go or similar). Check how it currently picks which project file to parse (e.g. package.json vs composer.json) when multiple exist in the same directory, and whether there's already a priority/list mechanism, to assess how hard it'd be to add a priority order or "show all" option.

For each of the 3, give: file path(s), current behavior in ~2-3 sentences, and a feasibility verdict (easy/moderate/hard) with the concrete reason (e.g. "just add a slice field and loop" vs "requires new architecture").

Keep total response under 400 words.

content.content:
Path segment: Multiple non-consecutive capture groups

### Code of Conduct

- [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What would you like to see changed?

I know this feature was implemented only very recently, but I still found something that could be improved. (Sorry!)

## Existing Behavior
At the present moment, the path segment regex replaces the first capture group found with the corresponding value in the config, leaving the rest of the string completely untouched.
https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/blob/main/src/segments/path.go#L639

This (while consistent with `mapped_locations`' preservation of subsequent portions of the path) presents an issue: what if you need something more than a mere 1 location substitution?

## The Situation

My situation is similar to the one in my prior issues, with an enclosing github folder I want replaced with an icon:
```json
"mapped_locations": {
	"/mnt/c": "C:/",
	"re:(.*/Users/taylo/Documents/GitHub).*": "github-icon"
}
```
This works fine in and of itself.

However, I place all my git worktrees for the current repo in a separate folder to keep track (`repoName.worktrees/branch-name`), and would like to have `oh-my-posh` additionally display "repoName" for these folders[^1]. 
In other words, I want something like this: 
```
C:/Users/taylo/Documents/GitHub/repoName.worktrees/branch-name/XXX
```
to turn into this:
```
github-icon/repoName/XXX
```

Unfortunately, there appears to be no way to do these 2 things simultaneously while preserving the original path and successive sub-elements. Given the lack of variable expansion in [regexp.FindStringSubmatch](https://pkg.go.dev/regexp#Regexp.FindStringSubmatch), neither of these work: 
```json
	"re:(.*/Users/taylo/Documents/GitHub/(?P<repoName>.*).worktrees/.*)": "github-icon/$repoName"
	"re:(.*/Users/taylo/Documents/GitHub/(.*).worktrees/.*)": "github-icon/$2"
```
While doing `regexp.Expand` could solve this, the existing code for finding submatches would have to be changed to use indices (not to mention 3 or more replacements gets you into some some really ugly nested capture groups).

## Proposals

After all that context, I have 3 main ideas for potential solutions to this (in no particular order):
1. Allow regex mapped location to replace successive capture groups with the corresponding values. 
  - Pros: 
    - Simple for users and fairly consistent with existing implementation.
  - Cons:
    - Questionable how to represent it inside config and/or JSON schema without breaking existing configs or changing implementations. Having a special delimiter sequence (comma, colon, etc.) risks breaking mappings containing those sequences, and allowing an array of values for this specific purpose would raise issues with how to interpret it for *normal* mapped locations.
    - Nested capture groups would seem to pose an interesting dilemmna - what do we replace if we have mappings for both.
    - IDK how you'd implement it in an idiomatic way that doesn't involve calling `strings.Replace` repeatedly or changing existing function behavior.
2. Add a config boolean toggle (proposed name `mapped_locations_regex_replaceAll`) to make values for regex patterns be treated as *literal replacement expressions* passed directly to [regex.ReplaceAllString](https://pkg.go.dev/regexp#Regexp.ReplaceAllString)[^2].
  - Pros:
    - Codebase already contains a function to facilitate "regex replace all" in the `regexp` package (yay code reuse)
    - Should be easier to implement and has more extensibility for nerds like me.
  - Cons:
    - Inconsistent with normal replacement syntax for restrictions outside the capture group. A basic expression like `"re:(.*/Users/taylo/Documents/GitHub)/apple": "github-icon"` would end up becoming `"re:.*/Users/taylo/Documents/GitHub(/apple)": "github-icon$1"` (since we want to preserve the succeeding "/apple")
3. Go with what I proposed in #6166 - adding a toggle to check all mapping lines and perform each replacement in turn. Execution order could probably be the exact reverse of normal (shortest first to ensure prefixes get mapped first)[^3].
  - Pros:
    - Keeps existing syntax while also allowing for more complex use cases (albeit with a few hoops)
  - Cons:
    - Probably not terribly performant to perform `regex.FindStringSubmatch` and `strings.Replace` once per mapping (even if it would make said mappings somewhat less complex). Whether it would be any less performant than using several capture groups and replacement patterns (or whether any change would actually make a noticeable difference) is up for debate.

## Notes

I'm fairly impartial on what solution is finally implemented, though I tried to keep my suggestions backwards compatible with existing configs (seen as this is a feature already in the wild). 
Thanks for reading. 

[^1]: I already have the git segment in my config, so showing the extra folders is just wasting my already fairly limited terminal space.
[^2]: I doubt adding an additional prefix (`rer:` or whatnot) solely for these regexes is a good idea.
[^3]: I know the issue was closed as completed earlier, but *it's merely an idea*.==========
repaint_prompt function for other shells

### Code of Conduct

- [ ] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What would you like to see added?

The shell integration for `fish` offers the function to [repaint the current prompt](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/faq#fish-display-current-bind-vim-mode). It would be nice if this was available for other shells too in order to allow for more scripting capabilities.

The shell integration for `fish` offers the function to [repaint the current prompt](https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/faq#fish-display-current-bind-vim-mode). It would be nice if this was available for other shells too in order to allow for more scripting capabilities.

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>## 1. `extends` chaining (issue 7531)

**File:** `src/config/load.go:119-136` (`Parse`), merge logic in `src/config/merge.go:50-115` (`Config.merge`).

Multi-hop chaining **already works today** — it's not a single-level feature. `Parse` runs `for cfg.Extends != "" { ... base.merge(cfg); cfg = base }`, so A→B→C resolves fully: read A, read B (A's extends), merge A onto B, then loop again on B's own `Extends` field (preserved via `nextExtends := cfg.Extends` in `merge()`, config.go:63 field, merge.go:55/111) and repeat with C, etc.

**Gap:** there's no cycle/loop detection — if A extends B extends A, the loop has no visited-set guard; it would only stop on a read error, not on a circular reference.

**Verdict: easy.** Chaining works; the only real gap is adding a `visited map[string]bool` (or similar) check in the `for cfg.Extends != ""` loop at load.go:119 to break on cycles — a small, contained addition, not new architecture.

## 2. Color cycle exclusion (issue 5442)

**Files:** `src/color/cycle.go` (`Cycle.Loop()`), `src/config/config.go:72` (`Cycle color.Cycle` field on `Config`), `src/prompt/primary.go:64` (`cycle = &e.Config.Cycle`), `src/prompt/segments.go:185-195` (`writeSegment`).

Cycle is a single package-level global `*color.Cycle` (segments.go:18, `var cycle *color.Cycle`) populated once from `Config.Cycle`. In `writeSegment`, every rendered, non-pending segment unconditionally gets `cycle.Loop()` applied — Foreground/Background are overwritten if the cycle is non-empty (segments.go:191-195), with no existing per-segment/block opt-out check.

**Verdict: easy/moderate.** Adding a per-segment (or per-block) bool field, e.g. `ExcludeFromCycle`, on the `Segment`/`Block` struct and a short-circuit `if !segment.ExcludeFromCycle { ... }` around segments.go:191 is a small, contained change — no architectural rework since the cycle logic is already centralized in one place.

## 3. Project segment multi-file (issue 7594)

**File:** `src/segments/project.go`, `Enabled()` (lines 75-164), `ProjectItem` struct (lines 26-30).

There's already a priority mechanism: `n.projects` is an ordered slice of `ProjectItem{Name, Files, Fetcher}`, iterated in a fixed, hardcoded order (node → deno → jsr → cargo → python → mojo → php/composer → dart → nuspec → dotnet → julia → lake → powershell), and the loop returns on the **first** match (`hasProjectFile` + `return true` at line 160). Each item's file list is independently overridable via `<name>_files` option (line 146-147), but the item ordering itself is not configurable, and there's no "show all matches" aggregation — only first-match wins.

**Verdict: moderate.** Adding a configurable priority order (e.g. an option listing `projects` names to reorder/filter `n.projects` before the loop at line 144) is straightforward since the ordered-slice structure already exists. A "show all" mode is harder: it requires changing `ProjectData`/`Template()` to hold multiple results and updating the default template (line 167) to iterate them — touches the render contract, not just selection logic.</result>

</task-notification>

lastPrompt: https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues which of the issues are feasible to solve?

content.text:
I reviewed all 6 open issues in [JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues) against the actual code (not just the request text). Here's the feasibility ranking:

## Easy

**[#7531](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7531) — Multi-level `extends`**
Already works today. [load.go:119](src/config/load.go) loops `for cfg.Extends != ""`, merging each level and chasing the next `Extends` field, so A→B→C already resolves. The only real gap is a missing cycle guard (A extends B extends A would loop forever / until a read error). Fix is a small `visited` set in that loop — this could largely be closed by demonstrating it works and adding cycle detection.

**[#5442](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/5442) — Per-block/segment color-cycle exclusion**
Cycle logic is centralized in one place — `writeSegment` in [segments.go:185-195](src/prompt/segments.go) unconditionally calls `cycle.Loop()` on every rendered segment. Adding an opt-out bool field (e.g. `exclude_from_cycle`) on `Segment`/`Block` plus a short-circuit there is small and contained — no architecture change needed.

## Moderate

**[#7594](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7594) — Project segment, multiple project files**
[project.go](src/segments/project.go) already iterates an ordered `[]ProjectItem` slice and stops at first match — the ordered-list structure needed for a configurable priority already exists, so exposing that order as a config option is straightforward. A "show all matches" mode is harder — it touches the template/render contract (`ProjectData` would need to hold multiple results), not just the selection logic.

**[#6334](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/6334) — Path segment, multiple capture groups**
Confirmed in [path.go:703-726](src/segments/path.go): `handleRegex` hardcodes `regex.FindStringMatch(pattern, input, 1)` (capture group index 1 only) then does a plain `strings.Replace`. Swapping this for `regexp.ReplaceAllString`-style expansion (`$1`, `$2`, …) is contained to this one function — the issue author's own option 2 is basically a rewrite of these ~20 lines. The hard part isn't code, it's picking a config syntax that doesn't break existing single-group configs (the maintainer would need to weigh in on that design choice).

**[#7427](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7427) — `repaint_prompt` for other shells**
Fish already has this (`omp_repaint_prompt` in [omp.fish:746](src/shell/scripts/omp.fish)). It's not one change but N — each shell needs its own hook into that shell's native redraw mechanism (zsh `reset-prompt`/`precmd`, pwsh PSReadLine, bash has no clean equivalent). Mechanically repetitive rather than deep, but touches 4-5 shell script files individually. Also: the maintainer already asked in the thread for a clearer use-case before investing the effort — worth confirming that's resolved before starting.

## Harder

**[#7572](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7572) — Go templates in palette colors**
Palette resolution ([palette.go](src/color/palette.go)) is a flat static string lookup (`p:` prefix chase), not template execution — unlike segment templates or path's `mapped_locations` keys (which already call `template.Render`). `PaletteColors.ToAnsi` ([colors.go:331](src/color/colors.go)) only receives a color string today, not the render context (segment/status data) that templates need for `.Segments.Contains(...)`. Feasible in principle since color resolution already happens per-segment at render time (so `.Segments` state from earlier segments would be available), but it requires threading a render-context object through the color-resolution path that doesn't currently carry one. More design work than the others.

**Recommendation:** #7531 (extends cycle detection) and #5442 (cycle exclusion) are the best starting points — small, contained, no design ambiguity. Want me to pick one and start implementation?

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\jande\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability:

| Role         | Capability tier                               | Owns                                      |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| Orchestrator | Strongest reasoning model available           | Analysis, plan, supervision, verification |
| Implementer  | Mid-tier coder; small model for trivial edits | Executing one pinned, self-contained task |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs on an implementer-tier model, skip delegation: plan and
execute directly, still following every phase. Never delegate to a model more capable than the
current one.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   cheapest capable executor.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

content.content:
1	# Phase 1 — Analyze
2	
3	Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
4	No code gets written or edited during this phase.
5	
6	## Gather the full context
7	
8	- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
9	  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
10	- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
11	  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
12	- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
13	  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).
14	
15	## Reproduce before theorizing
16	
17	Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
18	fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
19	hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.
20	
21	## Find the root cause in the code
22	
23	- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
24	  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
25	- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
26	  why it crashes.
27	- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.
28	
29	## Output of this phase
30	
31	A short analysis report to the user containing:
32	
33	1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
34	2. The proposed change and its scope.
35	3. What is intentionally out of scope.
36	4. Open questions, if any remain.
37	
38	## Stop gate
39	
40	Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
41	already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with

file.content:
# Phase 1 — Analyze

Start every task here, no matter how it arrived: issue link, PR number, verbal idea, bug report.
No code gets written or edited during this phase.

## Gather the full context

- Issues and PRs: `gh issue view <n> --comments` / `gh pr view <n> --comments`, plus linked
  issues, referenced discussions, and any code the report points at.
- Ideas and verbal requests: restate the goal and constraints in your own words. If the request
  is ambiguous, resolve the ambiguity now — not halfway through implementation.
- Check for prior art: existing helpers, similar segments/modules, and past commits that touched
  the same area (`git log -- <path>`).

## Reproduce before theorizing

Reproduce the problem when possible. A reproduction turns the analysis from a hypothesis into a
fact and gives Phase 5 its verification case for free. When reproduction is impossible (platform,
hardware, credentials), say so explicitly in the report and mark the fix as unverified-by-repro.

## Find the root cause in the code

- Read the actual implementation. Never reason from the issue text, a review comment, or a stack
  trace alone — reports and bot reviewers are frequently wrong.
- Distinguish the root cause from the symptom. Fixing where it crashes is not the same as fixing
  why it crashes.
- State what the change should be, which files it touches, and what it deliberately leaves alone.

## Output of this phase

A short analysis report to the user containing:

1. What is actually happening and why (root cause, with file references).
2. The proposed change and its scope.
3. What is intentionally out of scope.
4. Open questions, if any remain.

## Stop gate

Report the analysis and wait for a go before implementing. Skip the gate only when the user
already gave the go in the request itself ("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with
Sonnet"). A go given for analysis is not a go for implementation.

17	  fixes. Everything the workflow forbids delegating runs here.

19	  with decisions already made. Falls over when asked to make judgment calls, which is exactly why
20	  the spec removes them.

22	  tweaks, typo fixes, doc touch-ups. Batch several to amortize the dispatch overhead. When in

file.content:
# Model tiers and agent tooling

The workflow talks about capability tiers, not vendor names. Map whatever stack is in use onto
these three tiers. The model names below are a mid-2026 snapshot — they go stale; when a name no
longer exists, map its replacement by tier, not by nostalgia.

## The three tiers

| Tier         | Role                | Anthropic         | OpenAI            | Google            |
| ------------ | ------------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- | ----------------- |
| Orchestrator | Analysis, oversight | Fable 5, Opus 4.8 | Sol, GPT-5 (high) | Gemini 2.5 Pro    |
| Implementer  | Pinned-spec work    | Sonnet 5          | GPT-5, GPT-4.1    | Gemini 2.5 Flash  |
| Trivial      | Mechanical edits    | Haiku 4.5         | GPT-4.1 mini      | Gemini Flash-Lite |

- **Orchestrator** — the strongest reasoning model available. Pays for itself on root-cause
  analysis, architectural judgment, reviewing implementer diffs, and catching wrong-but-plausible
  fixes. Everything the workflow forbids delegating runs here.
- **Implementer** — the capable code monkey: fast, cheap, and reliable when handed a pinned spec
  with decisions already made. Falls over when asked to make judgment calls, which is exactly why
  the spec removes them.
- **Trivial** — small, very fast models for mechanical, unambiguous edits: renames, config
  tweaks, typo fixes, doc touch-ups. Batch several to amortize the dispatch overhead. When in
  doubt between trivial and implementer, pick implementer — a wrong cheap edit costs more than
  the price difference.

## Tooling mappings

How to run the orchestrator/implementer split in common agent tooling:

- **Claude Code** — the orchestrator session delegates via subagents (`Agent` tool) with a model
  override per task (`sonnet`, `haiku`), using worktree isolation for parallel tasks.
- **GitHub Copilot** — do analysis in Copilot Chat on the strongest reasoning model in the model
  picker; switch agent mode to a cheaper model for executing the pinned spec. Standalone,
  well-specified tasks can be assigned to the Copilot coding agent (assign the issue or PR to
  Copilot), which works on its own branch — the pinned spec becomes the issue body.
- **Codex** — orchestrate locally (CLI or chat) on the frontier model; dispatch each pinned spec
  as a Codex cloud task, one task per independent unit, and review the resulting diffs as PRs.
- **Cursor and similar IDE agents** — one composer/agent session per task with the model chosen
  per tier; background agents for the parallel ones.

**No subagent support at all?** Keep the phases, drop the parallelism: run analysis on the
strongest model, then switch the session model down for implementation, then switch back up to
review and verify. The discipline transfers even when the delegation mechanism does not.

input.prompt:
You are the orchestrator (analysis phase) for a Go CLI project, oh-my-posh, at D:\oh-my-posh (prompt theme engine). You're following this project's "code-changes" skill workflow, Phase 1 (Analyze). Do NOT write or edit any code in this phase — read-only investigation only.

## The issue

GitHub issue #7594, "project segment - multiple project files":

> I work on projects with multiple project files (eg. composer.json and package.json in the same directory). Only one of them has all the info in it, and I would like to have a way of showing the right one or all of them. There is currently no setting to tell what file should have priority or to loop over each.

No comments on the issue yet.

## What's already known (from a prior scouting pass — verify it yourself, don't trust it blindly)

The project segment lives in src/segments/project.go. A prior pass claimed:
- `n.projects` is an ordered slice of `ProjectItem{Name, Files, Fetcher}` structs, iterated in a fixed hardcoded order (node, deno, jsr, cargo, python, mojo, php/composer, dart, nuspec, dotnet, julia, lake, powershell), and the `Enabled()` method (roughly lines 75-164) returns on the FIRST match via `hasProjectFile`.
- Each item's file list is independently overridable via a `<name>_files` option.
- There is no way to reorder priority, and no "show all matches" aggregation.

## Your task

1. Read src/segments/project.go in full (and any related test file, e.g. project_test.go) to verify or correct the above claims with exact line numbers.
2. Determine root cause / gap: why can't a user today get both composer.json and package.json data, or control which wins?
3. Propose a concrete, backward-compatible change. Consider: (a) a config option to set/reorder priority among the existing project types, and (b) whether an "detect all present project files, expose all, template picks" mode is realistic given the current `ProjectData`/`Template()` structure, or if that requires changing the render contract. Recommend ONE approach — the one that's smallest and most backward-compatible while solving the actual reported need (choosing/prioritizing when multiple project files coexist in one directory).
4. Note explicitly what should be left out of scope.
5. List any open questions a maintainer would need to answer before implementation (e.g. exact config field name/shape, default behavior).

## Output format (this is exact — the orchestrator will paste it into a user-facing report)

```
### Issue #7594 — project segment multiple project files

**Root cause / gap:** ...(with file:line references)...

**Proposed change:** ...(concrete: what field(s)/struct changes, what file(s), what the config looks like)...

**Out of scope:** ...

**Open questions:** ...
```

Keep the whole response under 350 words. Be concrete and cite file:line, not vague descriptions.
